chapters.title: A clear look at who we really are
chapters.text:
We tend to believe that freedom is having lots of mental space to do whatever we want, the power to create variables. Nothing could be further from the truth. The abundance of options actually overwhelms us and leaves out even a hint of freedom. So, what is freedom then. The answer is simple. Discipline.

Discipline is the most honest mirror we own.

Getting a grip on ourselves is the best and the hardest skill we can develop. There is no mediator between us and our weaknesses, so there is no one to blame when we lose but ourselves. After hitting a brick wall, we have only one solution. We have to toughen up from the inside.

The self-development journey is never simple, and the odds are rarely in our favor. Jocko Willink is aware of the stakes and has some advice to share. For him, accepting hardship as a necessary evil is the first step toward personal victory. He says difficulties come in many forms, like criticism from others, mistakes, and procrastination, and we have to accept them before we can make the first move.

Physical training, which gauges our discipline, is one of the most challenging activities we can do. Luckily for us, Willink offers ways to grow our resilience, most of which come from our own nature. In a sense, building discipline the Willink way is returning to our roots. That said, we should not measure our physical capabilities against anyone else. Health always comes first, or we would not need to work on our strength. Always check your fitness before following any advice, including the bits in this summary.

Discipline can seem like your worst enemy. But in reality, it is your best friend.

And now that we have taken all the precautions, let us dive into the secrets of tomorrow success.

chapters.title: The battle you fight with yourself
chapters.text:
Fighting an outside enemy is easier than it sounds, because your whole self gets mobilized and motivated against the danger. But what do you do when the threat is yourself. You want to wake up earlier and start the day feeling fresh, but nine times out of ten, that idea is just wishful thinking, a safe zone that lets you believe you are stronger than you really are. In our minds, we are alone with our thoughts, and there is no one to help us fight our demons but us.

One enemy that is impossible to contend with is the self. That is why we fight every day.

We go to bed each night promising to fight better tomorrow. In other words, we talk about it instead of doing it. Discipline is the weapon we have to keep polishing with our actions, which is why even a tiny step can be more helpful than doing nothing. Cut the talk out of your strategy and use actions, because that is where the results are.

If you think you are disciplined because you go to the gym three times a week or finish a task just in time, ask yourself if you would do it if you were alone. External discipline can fool us into believing we have a strong will when, in reality, we are driven by the fear of disappointment or punishment. Self-discipline, on the other hand, is the ability to be your own supervisor, detached from compassion and leniency.

Speaking of detachment, stress can be a powerful demotivator from which we should learn to distance ourselves. We cannot control life circumstances, but our reaction is malleable, so why not use it to our advantage. Rather than giving in to stress, let us look at it from a different angle and see it as a chance to improve.

Obviously, we cannot succeed at something after one try and then sit back to rest. Failures and weaknesses are inevitable, but they are also our chance to train our minds to do the things we need but not necessarily want. After seeing the first results of our perseverance, it becomes easier to use our logic when our emotions tell us to stop. It also works the other way. Our anger and zeal must drive us forward when we see no point in enduring.

Fight weak emotions with the power of logic. Fight the weakness of logic with the power of emotion.

chapters.title: Do not say inspiration in this house
chapters.text:
Inspiration must be the biggest lie we hear about success. It is a rare and fleeting guest, and we use it to justify our passivity. Self-discipline, on the other hand, offers a long-lasting effect, but we have to pay for it with our effort.

During his career in the military, Willink worked with soldiers of different backgrounds, wealthy and blue-collar, Ivy League and community college graduates, two-parent and single-parent families, and he concluded that neither nature nor nurture ultimately shapes our selves. We cultivate the conscious choice that later helps us shape ourselves into who we want to be. To do that, we first have to win over our minds. In other words, we have to develop discipline.

The staples of self-control are these. Focus. Being afraid of failure. Learning from mistakes and misfortune. Beating the criticism. Turning procrastination into motivation.

The common problem most people face is being short-sighted about their goals. We crave immediate results to fuel motivation, but long-lasting results demand continuous input, so we should stop relying on fast carbohydrates and consume sustainable encouragement. It means keeping focus at all times and not giving up after a small victory.

A useful tool for staying intentional is being afraid of failure. We try our best when we do not want to mess things up. And even when it happens, we can learn from our mistakes. We should aim for the best but expect the worst, because that is where the best preparation lies.

Procrastination and criticism are simply trash we can recycle to get organic fuel.

While it all sounds good and smooth, external criticism often gets in the way of progress. Luckily, human psychology works in a way where we can turn a negative reaction into a positive one. Ask those who badmouth you for honest feedback and use it as leverage if it is constructive criticism. At last, we can always learn to ignore it, trusting our own compass.

Surprisingly, procrastination can also become a stimulus. Let us admit it. We will need it more than sugarcoated inspiration. When we drag our legs for a morning run despite not feeling like it, the motivation spike we feel afterward is greater than any external validation. It means we won over the most stubborn of all enemies, which is ourselves.

chapters.title: The perks of being a stressaholic
chapters.text:
Stress is an essential part of life that enables mental and physical growth. Years ago, harsh climates and wild animals pushed people to survive and eventually evolve. If they had avoided those stressors, humankind would have died out. While avoiding stress is counterproductive, the right amount of it can be helpful. Knowing how to use it is the key.

If not for stress, we would not appreciate satisfaction.

Physical training is a primary way to accumulate reasonable strain. Doing sports increases blood flow to the brain, improving neural exchange and cognition. This fact also helps with mental health, lifting mood and reducing harmful stress.

Speaking of the latter, too much strain keeps cortisol levels high, which raises blood pressure and can cause hypertension. Sports can turn harmful stress into positive stress, training the body and mind to balance cortisol levels. It does not matter what kind of physical activity you choose. The idea is to move, even when injured. Willink recommends doing whatever you can, even a simple walk, to stay active. You can start with basic equipment like a pull-up bar, gymnastic rings, or a squat rack.

Sleep is another way to control stress. A healthy sleep period runs between seven to nine hours. Willink preaches early waking, when the rest of the world is still asleep, to give us a psychological win. Waking at 5 am and heading straight to the gym kicks off the day, lowering the chance of grabbing a quick breakfast or junky lunch. As a result, we are more energized for work and social events. But to get enough sleep and become early birds, we have to be in bed at 9:30 pm or 10 pm. It seems impossible in today routine, but let us look at it from this angle. We straddle the inflexible regime by practicing discipline. We create our own rules, even if we need to change ourselves.

Early sleep is challenging, but we can stimulate it by getting tired through physical activity, which forces us to recharge, and by waking up early, which does the same trick. We can turn off devices and grab a book, since the light from phones and computers creates the illusion of daytime and stimulates the brain, while reading from a book calms it. We can avoid skipping weekends by getting up early every day of the week to establish the cycle and cement the habit.

chapters.title: Self-protection is also self-care
chapters.text:
In the modern survival list, self-defense comes in bold. We have to care for our economic well-being and health, and unfortunately, today world is not always safe. Learning self-defense is a worthwhile investment.

The martial arts are increasingly popular among people who are not professionally engaged in sports. Willink suggests several options suitable for beginners. Brazilian jiu-jitsu. Boxing. Muay Thai. Wrestling.

When we cannot escape danger, Brazilian jiu-jitsu provides a first-line defense when an assailant grips us. It has few basic moves, so the risk of getting overwhelmed is low. The main task in Brazilian jiu-jitsu is to destabilize the attacker and run away. In boxing, Willink highlights the maximum use of your hands so that you can protect yourself without additional equipment. Muay Thai is the next-level option that involves our fists, elbows, and knees, letting us strike with full potential. Wrestling trains our body and mind and teaches us to take initiative in a fight. We have to remember that all martial arts are mentally demanding, so prior preparation is necessary.

When we find a place to train, we should take a lesson as soon as possible to assess the future cost. The atmosphere is decisive in every learning environment, including martial arts. Some gyms practice the traditional routine of wearing uniforms and bowing to teachers, while others are less strict. Your choice should rely on your comfort or your willingness to endure some mental discipline. Willink also warns against dojos with a cult-like culture that focuses on an unhealthy obsession with the ability to hurt others.

Violence is the hair of the dog, not the means to an end.

Aside from martial arts, another way to protect ourselves is firearms. Willink stresses the importance of careful handling with four rules. Think of every gun as loaded. Do not point the gun at something you do not need to shoot. Your finger should stay off the trigger until the decisive moment. Notice your target and the people behind it.

High-quality training should develop your speed, focus, and instincts while teaching you to relax and control your breathing. Keep that in mind when choosing a trainer.

chapters.title: The breadcrumbs our ancestors left behind
chapters.text:
Food is the fuel for our bodies, a fact that helped our ancestors survive. Just like a car needs good-quality gas to work properly, we need a healthy diet to function.

To understand the cause-and-effect relationship, let us look at the homeostasis process. The body is healthy when all its systems function accordingly, meaning any imbalance dissolves in time through hormones. For example, eating sugar causes insulin release to regulate blood glucose levels. When sugar intake is too high, this hormone stores it in fat cells, causing weight gain. By this rule, to lose weight, our body must first use the sugar in the blood and then switch to stored sugar. To help the body use up the immediate sugar stock, we can exercise, fast, or reduce our carbohydrate intake.

Regular insulin spikes, triggered by too much sugar, can cause insulin resistance, which is, put simply, a road to type 2 diabetes. Aside from that, such a diet can provoke kidney and heart issues and even blindness.

Sugar is a carbohydrate, and carbohydrates are organic compounds our bodies have not fully adjusted to yet despite millennia of evolution. For example, humans started storing and consuming grains, which are essential carbohydrates, only around 20,000 years ago. Our bodies still struggle with digesting them, and they end up turning into sugar. With that in mind, Jocko Willink recommends the Paleolithic, or so-called caveman, diet, to give the body nutrients it can digest. The menu is relatively simple. Poultry. Beef. Eggs. Fish. Vegetables. Nuts. Some dairy products, like full-fat butter, yogurt, or cheese, but no milk. Some fruits, like apples, citruses, berries, bananas, kiwis, melons, and similar. Fungi.

Diet is more about will and mindset than physiology.

Conversely, grains, potatoes, refined sugar and salt, legumes, and processed oils are not part of the caveman diet. According to Willink, that menu almost excludes carbohydrates, enriching the body with fat and protein, the essential nutrients for survival. However, scientists have found many inconsistencies with that way of eating, so if you decide to try it, discuss the risks with your doctor.

Fasting is a safer option, with proven health benefits like better cognition, weight balance, and lower risk of inflammation. Choose the optimal intensity for you and fast regularly for visible results.

A quick note. Often, we confuse thirst with hunger. If you just had lunch, sip some water and wait for satiation.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Without self-discipline, planning an offensive against an outside enemy is easier than realizing a single goal, a simple truth that Jocko Willink has distilled throughout his years in the military. In our bodies fight for homeostasis, they sometimes play games with our minds. But we cannot force ourselves to change because we secretly want the old and familiar order. That is the reason our biggest enemy is ourselves. We want to become better versions of who we are but are not ready to face the challenges.

Willink argues that deep within ourselves, we are strong-willed. We have to become the personal strict mentor who attacks all our fears before they attack us, without relying on short spurts of inspiration. We are strong enough to recycle negative feedback or circumstance into fuel to move forward and achieve goal after goal.

Physical training and a healthy diet are the ultimate challenges we encounter in one way or another. In Willink philosophy, they are a chance to set a foundation for further victories. Even sleep can become a weapon if we learn to use it to our advantage. All it takes is a decision. In other words, we cannot compromise with the demons in our heads telling us to take a rest. We may deserve it, but it will not do us much good.

Either way, life is a strategy with free choices and actions. One thing to keep in mind. No one will win our battles for us.

A small exercise to try. Develop a joyous morning routine, so you will want to wake up early. Inspiration develops in the process of doing. Start working and watch yourself get engaged. Find a role model with a similar background or values to give you a perspective of the future you want.
